By integrating clinical, phosphoproteomic, and bioinformatic analyses, they identify and validate subtype-specific prognostic biomarkers and actionable therapeutic targets, exemplified by MPP7 in serous carcinoma.
implementation_steps: {'1': 'Acquire and process EOC and control FFPE tissue samples, confirming histology and clinical annotations.', '2': 'Extract and digest proteins; perform LC-MS/MS label-free proteomic profiling under consistent QC.', '3': 'Preprocess data: remove low-coverage proteins, normalize LFQ intensities, and impute missing values.', '4': 'Conduct differential expression analysis (Wilcoxon and limma), stratified by subtype and control.', '5': 'Perform GO, KEGG, Reactome, and hallmark enrichment analyses for DEPs.', '6': 'Construct protein co-expression network (wGCNA), identify modules, and annotate biological functions.', '7': 'Integrate phosphoproteomic data and annotate signaling pathway perturbations.', '8': 'Assess prognostic value (Kaplan-Meier/Cox) and druggability of candidate proteins per subtype.', '9': 'Validate candidate biomarkers/targets (e.g., IFIT3, MPP7) with PRM and functional cell assays.', '10': 'Statistical analysis and cross-validation with external CPTAC and database resources.'}
data: Primary dataset: 239 formalin-fixed paraffin-embedded EOC tumor samples (80 CCC, 79 EC, 80 SC) and 30 control ovarian tissues from surgical resections (total n=269), all collected at Shengjing Hospital, China (2013-2019). Secondary validation: CPTAC ovarian cancer cohort (83 EOC, 20 CT) for cross-cohort protein validation. Supplementary data: ExoCarta/Vesiclepedia exosome protein lists, STRING protein-protein interactions, DGIdb drug-target annotations.
evaluation_metrics: {'Differential Protein Expression': 'Statistical significance of up-/down-regulation (Wilcoxon test, BH-adjusted P-value < 0.01, |log2FC| > 1)', 'Classification Performance': 'Area Under the Curve (AUC) from ROC analysis (mean AUC > 0.9 in discovery, >0.8 in CPTAC validation)', 'Survival Analysis': 'Hazard Ratios (HR), 95% Confidence Interval, Cox proportional hazards P-values, Kaplan-Meier log-rank P-values', 'Network Analysis': "Pearson correlation coefficients for protein-protein interactions (mean >0.64), overlap with STRING (Fisher's exact test)", 'Experimental Validation': 'Changes in cell viability, proliferation, apoptosis (CCK-8, flow cytometry, transwell), statistical significance (ANOVA, P<0.05)', 'Reproducibility': 'Consistency of protein quantitation in PRM validation and external datasets'}
expected_outcome: The study achieves (1) detailed mapping of >8000 proteins across EOC subtypes, (2) identification of >1000 subtype-specific dysregulated proteins and key pathways, (3) prognostic/diagnostic biomarkers (e.g., IFIT3, mean AUC >0.9, HR>2 for poor outcome), (4) actionable therapeutic targets validated in vitro (e.g., MPP7 knockdown reduces SC cell proliferation/migration and increases apoptosis, P<0.01), and (5) a publicly available proteomic resource enabling future molecular and translational studies in ovarian cancer.
related_work_test: {'Proteomic landscape of epithelial ovarian cancer': 'This study provides a multi-histotype proteomic profiling of epithelial ovarian cancer (EOC), enabling improved understanding of subtype-specific biology and suggesting avenues for targeted diagnosis and treatment; however, it may be limited by cohort diversity and sample size.', 'Integrated proteomics and scRNA-seq analyses of ovarian cancer reveal molecular subtype-associated cell landscapes and immunotherapy targets': 'Combines bulk proteomic data with single-cell RNA sequencing to map cellular heterogeneity in ovarian cancer, highlighting novel immunotherapy targets, but integration challenges and technical biases may affect resolution.', 'Unveiling histotype-specific biomarkers in ovarian cancer: towards precision medicine': 'Focuses on the identification of histotype-specific protein biomarkers for ovarian cancer using advanced proteomic methods, facilitating personalized therapy approaches; the study may be constrained by the availability of high-quality tissue samples.', 'Single-cell transcriptome analysis revealed heterogeneity in epithelial ovarian cancer': 'Utilizes single-cell transcriptomics to dissect cellular heterogeneity within EOC, uncovering distinct subpopulations and molecular signatures; while offering high-resolution insights, it may not fully capture proteomic level changes.', 'Elucidating the mechanistic role of ovarian cancer biomarkers via proteomic profiling': 'Applies targeted proteomic profiling to investigate the functional roles of specific biomarkers in ovarian cancer progression, providing mechanistic insights but limited by the scope of biomarker selection and pathway coverage.'}

question:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- Docherty et al. (2020): Developed a risk score for sudden cardiac death (SCD) post-myocardial infarction (MI) in patients with reduced left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF), incorporating numerous clinical variables, but the model is based on older cohorts and is cumbersome for contemporary clinical use.
- Faxen et al. (2020): Proposed the OHCA score predicting 90-day out-of-hospital cardiac arrest after MI using clinical variables, but lacked external validation and missed many non-resuscitated OHCA cases, limiting generalizability.
- Norby et al. (2023): Identified 26 circulating proteins associated with sudden cardiac arrest using mass spectrometry in a small cohort; predictive value was not externally validated.
- Andersson et al. (2020): Linked GDF-15 to SCD via proteomics but used a non-standard SCD definition and lacked external validation.
- Stampe et al. (2024): Reported two proteins related to ventricular fibrillation post-first STEMI; lacked external validation and did not assess predictive value.
- Traditional Biomarker Studies: Investigated single laboratory or plasma markers (e.g., NT-proBNP, CRP, haptoglobin) for SCD prediction, but demonstrated limited discrimination and inconsistent reproducibility.

###2. Challenge:
The core challenges in predicting sudden cardiac death (SCD) post-myocardial infarction (MI) stem from several technical limitations. Firstly, existing models predominantly rely on single-parameter or traditional clinical-variable-based approaches, resulting in low sensitivity and specificity, particularly among patients with preserved left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF). This limitation underscores the need for integrative models that can effectively utilize high-dimensional data. Secondly, many studies lack robust external validation, leading to concerns about the generalizability of findings across diverse clinical populations. For instance, risk scores like the OHCA score and various biomarker studies have not been validated in larger, independent cohorts, which hampers their clinical applicability. Additionally, the reliance on outdated cohorts in some models restricts their relevance to current patient demographics and treatment paradigms. Finally, the challenge of capturing the multifactorial nature of biological processes associated with SCD is exacerbated by the limited use of advanced high-throughput proteomics techniques, which could provide deeper insights into the pathophysiology of SCD. Overcoming these challenges requires innovative approaches that combine multiple biomarkers and advanced analytics with rigorous validation protocols to enhance predictive power and clinical utility.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Prior approaches predominantly employ single-parameter or clinical-variable–based models, showing low sensitivity and specificity, particularly in populations with preserved LVEF; they do not leverage high-throughput proteomics to capture early, multifactorial biological processes and lack robust prospective and external validation.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
Prompt and accurate identification of post-AMI patients at high risk for out-of-hospital SCD is critical for timely intervention but is greatly hampered by the limitations of LVEF-based stratification. The recognition that SCD arises from complex, multi-pathway processes motivates the need for a proteomics-driven, multi-marker strategy capable of capturing early molecular cues predictive of SCD risk.

###5. Task Objective:
To discover, validate, and clinically test a novel plasma protein-based multi-biomarker panel (SCD-W3P) for improved prediction of out-of-hospital sudden cardiac death post-myocardial infarction, surpassing current clinical and risk score models, with specific attention to the LVEF-preserved population.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- LVEF-Based Stratification: Uses left ventricular ejection fraction ≤35% as the principal criterion for ICD candidacy post-AMI, but demonstrates poor sensitivity—most SCDs occur in patients with preserved LVEF.
- Clinical Risk Scores (Docherty et al., Faxen et al.): Aggregate clinical parameters (age, heart rate, comorbidities) to estimate SCD risk, improving over LVEF alone but limited by model complexity, lack of biomarker integration, and poor validation across contemporary populations.
- Single Biomarker Approaches: Measure individual plasma proteins or markers (e.g., NT-proBNP, CRP, haptoglobin) for SCD risk, but lack robustness and discriminative power for clinical deployment.
- Early Proteomics Studies: Identify protein candidates using mass spectrometry in small, non-representative cohorts with limited or absent validation.

##Your Task:

Based on the context above, please generate the following sections for a new research proposal. Be specific, clear, and innovative. Please limit the generated idea to 500 characters.

###1. Idea:
(Based on the above information, please propose an innovative and feasible idea. Include the required professional methods, reasoning, and logical development. Please limit your proposal to 300-500 characters.)

###2. ImplementationSteps:
(Provide a complete and specific list of implementation steps for your idea, and number each step.)

###3. ImplementationOrder:
(Provide a formatted execution route and a checklist of implementation steps. For example, "1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5".)

###4. Dataset:
(Describe the dataset required for the evaluation. If it needs to be created, explain how.)

###5. EvaluationMetrics:
(Define specific, measurable metrics to evaluate the success of the project. Explain why these metrics are relevant.)

###6. ExpectedOutcome:
(Describe the anticipated results and their potential impact on the field. Compare them to the existing solutions.)

existing_solutions: {'LVEF-Based Stratification': 'Uses left ventricular ejection fraction ≤35% as the principal criterion for ICD candidacy post-AMI, but demonstrates poor sensitivity—most SCDs occur in patients with preserved LVEF.', 'Clinical Risk Scores (Docherty et al., Faxen et al.)': 'Aggregate clinical parameters (age, heart rate, comorbidities) to estimate SCD risk, improving over LVEF alone but limited by model complexity, lack of biomarker integration, and poor validation across contemporary populations.', 'Single Biomarker Approaches': 'Measure individual plasma proteins or markers (e.g., NT-proBNP, CRP, haptoglobin) for SCD risk, but lack robustness and discriminative power for clinical deployment.', 'Early Proteomics Studies': 'Identify protein candidates using mass spectrometry in small, non-representative cohorts with limited or absent validation.'}
keywords: C statistic (AUC, area under ROC curve)
core_idea: This study integrates high-throughput untargeted and targeted proteomics to identify and validate a 3-protein plasma biomarker panel (CFD, CORO1A, HP) for early SCD risk stratification post-AMI. The SCD-W3P model demonstrates superior discrimination and reclassification over established clinical and risk score models, including in patients with preserved LVEF, and its biological plausibility is supported by in vivo functional studies targeting CFD.
implementation_steps: {'1': 'Prospectively enroll AMI patients, collect plasma samples within 24h of admission, and adjudicate SCD endpoints via standardized follow-up.', '2': 'Define discovery and validation (internal and external) nested case-control cohorts using rigorous matching (age, sex, clinical features).', '3': 'Perform untargeted proteomics (DIA-MS) on subset samples to identify differentially expressed proteins (DEPs) associated with SCD.', '4': 'Conduct pathway and network enrichment analyses to elucidate key biological processes.', '5': 'Quantify candidate DEPs by targeted proteomics (PRM) in the full discovery cohort.', '6': 'Select optimal multi-protein biomarker panel using predictor selection classifiers via repeated logistic regression.', '7': 'Develop SCD risk prediction model (SCD-W3P) based on selected proteins, calculate risk scores for each patient.', '8': 'Validate SCD-W3P by ELISA-based quantification in two independent cohorts and compare with LVEF and established risk models.', '9': 'Assess model performance: discrimination (C statistic), calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow), reclassification (NRI/IDI), and decision curve analysis.', '10': 'Evaluate biological plausibility of key proteins (CFD) in SCD pathogenesis using gene knockout and pharmacological inhibition in mouse MI models.'}
data: Three nested case-control cohorts: (1) Discovery cohort—n=210 (105 SCD cases, 105 matched survivors) from 5,908 AMI patients at Harbin Medical University (2017–2020); (2) Validation cohort 1—n=234 (40 SCD cases, 194 survivors) from 5,769 AMI patients (2020–2023) at the same center; (3) External validation cohort 2—n=48 (24 SCD cases, 24 survivors) from 1,756 AMI patients at Fujian Provincial People's Hospital (2017–2021). All cohorts include prospectively collected plasma within 24h of AMI admission, rigorous clinical matching, and 2-year follow-up for SCD adjudication.
evaluation_metrics: {'Discrimination (C statistic/AUC)': "Measures the model's ability to differentiate between SCD cases and survivors; higher values indicate better discrimination.", 'Calibration (Hosmer-Lemeshow test, calibration plot)': 'Assesses agreement between predicted and observed risks.', 'Net Reclassification Improvement (NRI)': 'Evaluates improvement in correct risk classification by adding the biomarker model to existing models.', 'Integrated Discrimination Improvement (IDI)': 'Quantifies the improvement in average sensitivity and specificity.', 'Sensitivity/Specificity': 'Proportion of true positives/negatives correctly identified.', 'Decision Curve Analysis': 'Assesses net clinical benefit across threshold probabilities.', 'Conditional Logistic Regression': 'Confirms robustness of predictive performance accounting for matched design.'}
expected_outcome: The SCD-W3P model (CFD, CORO1A, HP) achieves robust SCD risk prediction post-AMI, with C statistic 0.75–0.76 across cohorts, significantly outperforming LVEF (C statistic ≈0.55–0.57) and established risk models. It yields marked NRI (up to 0.95 continuous NRI) and IDI (up to 0.29) improvements. The model is particularly effective for the LVEF-preserved population and is externally validated in two independent cohorts. Experimental inhibition of CFD in mice confers protection from post-MI mortality and arrhythmias, supporting the panel's biological relevance and potential for guiding intensive management.
related_work_test: {'Dynamic Biomarker Trajectories in the First 72 Hours After AMI-Induced Cardiac Arrest': 'This study evaluates the prognostic value of serial biomarker measurements within the first 72 hours following acute myocardial infarction (AMI)-induced cardiac arrest, highlighting the significance of dynamic changes over static values but limited to the immediate post-event window.', 'Quantification of Myocardial Biomarkers in Sudden Cardiac Death': 'Investigates the use of cardiac markers, particularly troponin I, in peripheral blood for postmortem diagnosis of sudden cardiac death (SCD), supporting their diagnostic value but focused on retrospective, rather than predictive, assessment.', 'Protein Biomarkers of Cardiovascular Disease and Mortality': 'Identifies numerous protein biomarkers predictive of cardiovascular outcomes and all-cause mortality, offering a broad proteomic perspective but not specifically tailored to post-MI sudden cardiac death.', 'A Panel of Blood Biomarkers Unique to Sudden Cardiac Arrest': 'Reports the identification of a panel of novel blood biomarkers specifically associated with sudden cardiac arrest, including markers outside the coronary artery disease pathway, though further validation is needed for clinical use.', 'Novel Biomarkers for Predicting Acute Myocardial Infarction': 'Utilizes proteomic techniques to discover new protein biomarkers for early diagnosis and risk prediction of acute myocardial infarction, emphasizing early detection capabilities but not directly focused on post-MI SCD.'}

question:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- Hoshino et al., Cell 2020: Large-scale proteomic profiling of extracellular vesicles (EVs) across multiple human cancers, identifying pan-cancer EV markers but lacking breast cancer–specific classifier development and functional validation.
- Rontogianni et al., Commun Biol 2019: EV proteomics for breast cancer subtyping via comparative mass spectrometry; provides subtype stratification but not direct diagnostic or therapeutic biomarker translation.
- Shen et al., IJMS 2023: Comparative proteomics of breast cancer–derived exosomes, identifying pathways and protein markers linked to metastasis, but limited clinical validation and absence of mechanistic intervention.
- Tang et al., Genome Med 2018: Proteotranscriptomics in breast cancer to associate protein-mRNA concordance with subtypes and survival; offers rich tissue-based targets but not serum EV-based diagnostic models.

###2. Challenge:
The core challenges in the research of extracellular vesicles (EVs) in breast cancer primarily revolve around three technical difficulties. Firstly, the sensitivity of current proteomic profiling techniques often falls short, resulting in incomplete characterization of EV protein compositions. This limits the ability to identify low-abundance biomarkers that could be critical in early cancer detection. Secondly, the lack of rigorous cross-cohort validation across diverse patient populations poses a significant obstacle to establishing the robustness and reliability of identified biomarkers. Without this validation, findings may not translate effectively to clinical settings. Thirdly, there is a notable gap in integrating functional analyses that link EV cargoes to metastatic progression and therapeutic responses. This disconnect hinders the understanding of the biological roles of EVs and their potential as therapeutic targets. Additionally, the current focus on tissue-based rather than serum-derived EVs restricts the development of practical diagnostic models that can be applied in non-invasive settings. Addressing these challenges is essential for enhancing the translational potential of EV research in breast cancer diagnostics and therapy.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Current studies primarily profile EV protein composition with limited sensitivity, lack rigorous cross-cohort validation, and rarely integrate functional analyses to link EV cargoes with metastatic progression or therapeutic targeting, thereby restricting translational potential and clinical utility.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
The increasing evidence for EVs as stable, abundant, and minimally invasive biomarkers in liquid biopsy underscores the urgent need for comprehensive, clinically robust, and functionally validated EV proteomic signatures to improve early breast cancer detection, metastatic risk assessment, and identification of new therapeutic targets.

###5. Task Objective:
To systematically characterize and validate the proteomic landscape of serum EVs in breast cancer patients, construct and validate EV protein-based classifiers for diagnosis and metastasis assessment, and identify and functionally validate EV-borne therapeutic targets for intervention.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- Hoshino et al.: Unbiased EV proteome profiling across cancers; reveals diagnostic EV protein patterns but lacks breast cancer–specific classifiers and functional target validation.
- Rontogianni et al.: EV proteome data allow subtype stratification via mass spectrometry; however, clinical diagnostic accuracy and intervention potential are not addressed.
- Shen et al.: Identification of metastasis-related exosomal markers and pathways; does not pursue classifier construction or therapeutic proof-of-concept.
- Tang et al.: Tissue proteome–transcriptome concordance and prognosis correlations; not focused on circulating EVs or noninvasive diagnostics.

##Your Task:

Based on the context above, please generate the following sections for a new research proposal. Be specific, clear, and innovative. Please limit the generated idea to 500 characters.

###1. Idea:
(Based on the above information, please propose an innovative and feasible idea. Include the required professional methods, reasoning, and logical development. Please limit your proposal to 300-500 characters.)

###2. ImplementationSteps:
(Provide a complete and specific list of implementation steps for your idea, and number each step.)

###3. ImplementationOrder:
(Provide a formatted execution route and a checklist of implementation steps. For example, "1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5".)

###4. Dataset:
(Describe the dataset required for the evaluation. If it needs to be created, explain how.)

###5. EvaluationMetrics:
(Define specific, measurable metrics to evaluate the success of the project. Explain why these metrics are relevant.)

###6. ExpectedOutcome:
(Describe the anticipated results and their potential impact on the field. Compare them to the existing solutions.)

motivation: The increasing evidence for EVs as stable, abundant, and minimally invasive biomarkers in liquid biopsy underscores the urgent need for comprehensive, clinically robust, and functionally validated EV proteomic signatures to improve early breast cancer detection, metastatic risk assessment, and identification of new therapeutic targets.
task_objective: To systematically characterize and validate the proteomic landscape of serum EVs in breast cancer patients, construct and validate EV protein-based classifiers for diagnosis and metastasis assessment, and identify and functionally validate EV-borne therapeutic targets for intervention.
existing_solutions: {'Hoshino et al.': 'Unbiased EV proteome profiling across cancers; reveals diagnostic EV protein patterns but lacks breast cancer–specific classifiers and functional target validation.', 'Rontogianni et al.': 'EV proteome data allow subtype stratification via mass spectrometry; however, clinical diagnostic accuracy and intervention potential are not addressed.', 'Shen et al.': 'Identification of metastasis-related exosomal markers and pathways; does not pursue classifier construction or therapeutic proof-of-concept.', 'Tang et al.': 'Tissue proteome–transcriptome concordance and prognosis correlations; not focused on circulating EVs or noninvasive diagnostics.'}
core_idea: This study creates a large-scale, data-independent acquisition (DIA)–based proteomic map of serum EVs in breast cancer, employs machine learning to derive robust diagnostic and metastatic classifiers, identifies the EV protein TALDO1 as a biomarker and functional driver of metastasis, and develops a novel allosteric inhibitor (AO-022) to therapeutically target TALDO1, validated both in vitro and in vivo.
implementation_steps: {'1': 'Serum collection from breast cancer patients and healthy donors following standard clinical protocols.', '2': 'Isolation and purification of EVs using sequential ultracentrifugation and size exclusion chromatography; verification by TEM, NTA, and immunoblotting.', '3': 'Protein extraction from purified EV samples and tryptic digestion.', '4': 'Global mass spectrometry proteomic profiling using DIA-LC-MS/MS.', '5': 'Raw data processing, peptide identification, and protein quantification using bioinformatics tools (Firmiana, FragPipe, DIANN).', '6': 'Statistical analysis to identify differentially expressed EV proteins and perform pathway enrichment analyses.', '7': 'Machine learning model construction (XGBoost, ExtraTrees) for diagnostic classifier and LN metastasis classifier training, internal cross-validation, and external cohort validation.', '8': 'Cross-referencing with external databases (Vesiclepedia, EVpedia, ExoCarta, tissue proteomes) for specificity and overlap analysis.', '9': 'Functional assays: in vitro (knockdown/overexpression of TALDO1, migration, invasion, EMT marker analysis), in vivo (xenograft tumor models, EV supplementation), and PDO-based viability assays.', '10': 'High-throughput virtual screening and molecular docking to identify potential TALDO1 inhibitors, followed by thermal shift assay and in vitro efficacy validation.', '11': 'Preclinical validation of the identified inhibitor (AO-022) in cell lines, xenograft models, and PDOs.', '12': 'Statistical evaluation and reporting of diagnostic metrics (AUC, sensitivity, specificity) and therapeutic efficacy.'}
data: The study comprises a discovery cohort (n=196; 126 breast cancer patients, 70 healthy donors) and five independent validation cohorts totaling 560 samples, including Fujian and two Shanghai serum EV cohorts, CPTAC breast cancer tissue proteomics (n=75), and Tang et al.'s breast cancer tissue proteomics (n=118). Data are derived from prospectively collected, treatment-naive patients with detailed clinical and metastatic annotation. All EVs are isolated from serum and rigorously validated for purity, and comprehensive proteomic data are publicly available.
evaluation_metrics: {'Diagnostic Performance': 'Area under the ROC curve (AUC), sensitivity, and specificity for distinguishing breast cancer from healthy donors and metastatic status.', 'Cross-validation': 'Five-fold cross-validation to assess model robustness and prevent overfitting.', 'External Validation': 'Performance of classifiers in independent cohorts (AUC, sensitivity, specificity).', 'Functional Validation': 'In vitro migration/invasion assays (percentage change), wound healing assays (migration rate), and in vivo tumor growth (volume, weight reduction).', 'Molecular Mechanism': 'Changes in EMT marker expression (E-cadherin, N-cadherin, Snail), NADPH/NADP+ ratio, nucleotide pool levels after intervention.', 'Therapeutic Efficacy': 'Reduction in tumor volume/weight in xenograft models, viability inhibition in PDO assays, with statistical significance (P-values).'}
expected_outcome: The EV proteomic classifiers achieve high diagnostic accuracy (AUC up to 0.98, >90% sensitivity and specificity) for breast cancer and LN metastasis across cohorts. TALDO1 is validated as a serum EV biomarker for distant metastasis and functional driver of invasion. The novel inhibitor AO-022 exhibits potent anti-metastatic effects in vitro and reduces tumor burden in vivo, supporting the translational potential of EV proteomics for minimally invasive diagnostics and targeted therapy in breast cancer.

While offering a broad overview of techniques and clinical potential, it notes challenges such as standardization and reproducibility.', 'Proteomic Characterization of Serum Small Extracellular Vesicles for Liquid Biopsy in Breast Cancer': "Xu et al. constructed specific protein identifiers from serum small EVs that differentiate breast cancer from benign conditions, demonstrating the feasibility of using EV proteomics as a liquid biopsy tool. However, the study's sample size and technical variability may affect generalizability.", 'Proteomic analysis of plasma exosomes reveals novel biomarkers of breast cancer': 'This work explores the proteomic landscape of plasma exosomes in breast cancer patients, identifying novel biomarker candidates and providing insights into disease mechanisms. While promising, clinical utility is limited by the need for larger validation cohorts and standardized protocols.'}

question:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- Saltz et al. (2018, 2021): Mapped tumor-infiltrating lymphocyte (TIL) abundance and distribution across 23 cancer types from H&E slides using deep learning, demonstrating the prognostic value of TILs but limited to immune cell spatial quantification.
- Graham et al. (2019) - Hover-Net: Developed a nuclei segmentation and classification network enabling single-cell quantification of tumor, stroma, and lymphocytes from H&E slides, yet requiring intensive pixel-level annotations.
- Diao et al. (2021): Applied supervised machine learning to quantify 607 human-interpretable TME features from histopathology images, but heavily reliant on labor-intensive expert annotations and lacking scalability to weakly labeled datasets.
- Hu et al. (2021): Employed deep learning to extract hidden features from histopathology images for anti-PD-1 response prediction in melanoma and lung cancer, offering low interpretability and modest predictive accuracy (AUROC: 0.645) for NSCLC.
- Wang et al. (2022): Used handcrafted spatial interaction features between tumor cells and TILs to predict immunotherapy response, achieving strong performance but requiring large tissue areas and limited generalizability to small biopsies.
- Park et al. (2022); Ding et al. (2022); Horst et al. (2024): Used automated deep learning or vision transformer–based tools for TIL counting and spatial analysis, showing prognostic utility but limited to immune cell density rather than full TME characterization.

###2. Challenge:
The core challenges in the research area of tumor microenvironment (TME) analysis using histopathology images stem from several technical obstacles. Firstly, the reliance on exhaustive expert annotations for training deep learning models impedes scalability and increases labor costs, particularly as existing methods often require pixel-level precision, which is not feasible in large-scale studies. Secondly, many approaches lack the ability to comprehensively characterize the TME's molecular and cellular diversity, focusing primarily on TIL density or specific markers like PD-L1, which limits their prognostic capabilities and reduces interpretability. Additionally, existing models often struggle to maintain predictive accuracy across varying tissue types and sizes, as exemplified by the challenges of applying handcrafted features to small biopsies, which are common in clinical settings. Finally, there is a significant gap in the integration of multi-modal data sources that could enhance the understanding of TME complexities, particularly in cases where biomarker expression is low or where tissue availability is constrained. Addressing these challenges is essential for developing robust, generalizable tools for TME assessment and predicting immunotherapy responses.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Prior methods predominantly focus on PD-L1 quantification or TIL density and require exhaustive expert annotations, thus failing to comprehensively assess TME molecular and cellular diversity or predict immunotherapy response with sufficient accuracy and interpretability, especially in scenarios with limited tissue availability and low biomarker expression.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
Given the pivotal role of the TME in modulating immunotherapy response and the inadequacy of existing predictors, there is a critical need for scalable, interpretable, and robust computational frameworks that can infer detailed TME composition, including cellular and molecular signatures, from routinely collected digital H&E pathology slides, thereby improving patient stratification and guiding immunotherapy decisions.

###5. Task Objective:
To develop and validate HistoTME, a weakly supervised, multi-task deep learning framework capable of inferring 30 cell type–specific TME molecular signatures from H&E digital pathology images of NSCLC, and to demonstrate its utility in predicting immunotherapy response and patient survival across large, independent cohorts.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- Saltz et al.: Deep learning for spatial mapping of TILs, providing immune infiltration quantification but lacking molecular or multi-cellular signature prediction.
- Hover-Net (Graham et al.): Nuclei segmentation/classification for cell-type quantification, requiring pixel-level annotation and not directly inferring molecular TME features.
- Diao et al.: Supervised ML on annotated images for interpretable TME features, limited by annotation scalability and lack of weak supervision.
- PD-L1 deep learning predictors: DL models for PD-L1 status from H&E, generally focused on single biomarkers and lacking comprehensive TME coverage.
- Spatial TIL-based predictors: Handcrafted spatial metrics for TILs predictive of response, effective but requiring extensive tissue and not generalizable to biopsies.

##Your Task:

Based on the context above, please generate the following sections for a new research proposal. Be specific, clear, and innovative. Please limit the generated idea to 500 characters.

###1. Idea:
(Based on the above information, please propose an innovative and feasible idea. Include the required professional methods, reasoning, and logical development. Please limit your proposal to 300-500 characters.)

###2. ImplementationSteps:
(Provide a complete and specific list of implementation steps for your idea, and number each step.)

###3. ImplementationOrder:
(Provide a formatted execution route and a checklist of implementation steps. For example, "1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5".)

###4. Dataset:
(Describe the dataset required for the evaluation. If it needs to be created, explain how.)

###5. EvaluationMetrics:
(Define specific, measurable metrics to evaluate the success of the project. Explain why these metrics are relevant.)

###6. ExpectedOutcome:
(Describe the anticipated results and their potential impact on the field. Compare them to the existing solutions.)

challenge: The core challenges in the research area of tumor microenvironment (TME) analysis using histopathology images stem from several technical obstacles. Firstly, the reliance on exhaustive expert annotations for training deep learning models impedes scalability and increases labor costs, particularly as existing methods often require pixel-level precision, which is not feasible in large-scale studies. Secondly, many approaches lack the ability to comprehensively characterize the TME's molecular and cellular diversity, focusing primarily on TIL density or specific markers like PD-L1, which limits their prognostic capabilities and reduces interpretability. Additionally, existing models often struggle to maintain predictive accuracy across varying tissue types and sizes, as exemplified by the challenges of applying handcrafted features to small biopsies, which are common in clinical settings. Finally, there is a significant gap in the integration of multi-modal data sources that could enhance the understanding of TME complexities, particularly in cases where biomarker expression is low or where tissue availability is constrained. Addressing these challenges is essential for developing robust, generalizable tools for TME assessment and predicting immunotherapy responses.
limitation: Prior methods predominantly focus on PD-L1 quantification or TIL density and require exhaustive expert annotations, thus failing to comprehensively assess TME molecular and cellular diversity or predict immunotherapy response with sufficient accuracy and interpretability, especially in scenarios with limited tissue availability and low biomarker expression.
motivation: Given the pivotal role of the TME in modulating immunotherapy response and the inadequacy of existing predictors, there is a critical need for scalable, interpretable, and robust computational frameworks that can infer detailed TME composition, including cellular and molecular signatures, from routinely collected digital H&E pathology slides, thereby improving patient stratification and guiding immunotherapy decisions.
task_objective: To develop and validate HistoTME, a weakly supervised, multi-task deep learning framework capable of inferring 30 cell type–specific TME molecular signatures from H&E digital pathology images of NSCLC, and to demonstrate its utility in predicting immunotherapy response and patient survival across large, independent cohorts.
existing_solutions: {'Saltz et al.': 'Deep learning for spatial mapping of TILs, providing immune infiltration quantification but lacking molecular or multi-cellular signature prediction.', 'Hover-Net (Graham et al.)': 'Nuclei segmentation/classification for cell-type quantification, requiring pixel-level annotation and not directly inferring molecular TME features.', 'Diao et al.': 'Supervised ML on annotated images for interpretable TME features, limited by annotation scalability and lack of weak supervision.', 'PD-L1 deep learning predictors': 'DL models for PD-L1 status from H&E, generally focused on single biomarkers and lacking comprehensive TME coverage.', 'Spatial TIL-based predictors': 'Handcrafted spatial metrics for TILs predictive of response, effective but requiring extensive tissue and not generalizable to biopsies.'}
keywords: feature engineering (sum, product, quotient of features)
core_idea: HistoTME leverages weakly supervised multi-task deep learning with foundation models and attention-based multiple instance learning to directly infer 30 TME molecular signatures from digital H&E slides, enabling robust, scalable, and interpretable prediction of immunotherapy response and survival in NSCLC, even from core biopsies, and complementing existing biomarkers like PD-L1.

data: The TCGA-NSCLC cohort (865 patients, 955 WSIs) with matched H&E slides and bulk RNA-seq data for model training; CPTAC-NSCLC cohort (333 patients, 1501 WSIs) for validation; SUNY Upstate clinical cohort (652 NSCLC patients, 1329 H&E slides, including 82 with matched IHC and 230 with annotated immunotherapy response), containing both surgical resections and core needle biopsies, along with comprehensive survival and treatment data.
evaluation_metrics: {'Pearson/Spearman Correlation': 'Measures linear and rank correlation between predicted TME signature expression and ground truth bulk transcriptomic or IHC data.', 'AUROC': 'Assesses the discrimination ability of response prediction models for immunotherapy (responder vs non-responder).', 'Sensitivity/Specificity/PPV': 'Quantifies true positive, true negative, and positive predictive value for responder classification.', 'Kaplan-Meier Survival Analysis': 'Estimates and compares overall/progression-free survival across predicted groups.', 'Hazard Ratio (Cox regression)': 'Quantifies relative risk of death or progression between predicted TME subgroups.', 'Youden Index': 'Identifies the optimal threshold for classification on the ROC curve.', 'Feature Importance (SHAP)': 'Quantifies contribution of each TME signature or interaction feature to response prediction.'}

question:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- Kather2019: Utilizes deep learning to predict microsatellite instability directly from gastrointestinal cancer histology images, demonstrating feasibility of histopathology-based biomarker prediction but focuses on MSI rather than TMB.
- Xu2019: Develops an automated whole slide image analysis pipeline for TMB prediction from bladder cancer pathology, leveraging transfer learning with deep CNNs to extract histological features; limited to bladder cancer and lacking systematic analysis for gastrointestinal cancer.
- Maron1998: Introduces the multi-instance learning (MIL) framework where bags are labeled based on the instance composition; widely adopted for large image problems but classical MIL does not fully utilize patch-level contributions in histopathology.

###2. Challenge:
The core challenges in the research area of image-based biomarker prediction for gastrointestinal cancers, specifically targeting tumor mutational burden (TMB), are multifaceted. First, there is a significant gap in the applicability of existing deep learning models, which predominantly focus on other biomarkers like microsatellite instability (MSI) or are limited to specific cancer types, such as bladder cancer, thus hindering generalization across diverse histological contexts. Second, the implementation of multi-instance learning (MIL) frameworks in histopathology remains suboptimal; classical MIL fails to effectively leverage patch-level contributions, which are critical for accurately representing the complex heterogeneity of tumor microenvironments. This results in a lack of precision in aggregate label assignments necessary for robust biomarker prediction. Additionally, the technical challenge of stain normalization is pivotal, as variations in staining can significantly affect feature extraction and model performance. Lastly, there is insufficient systematic evaluation of thresholding techniques for patient-level decisions, which complicates the translation of model predictions into clinically relevant outcomes. Addressing these challenges is essential for advancing the reliability and applicability of histopathology-based biomarker prediction methodologies in gastrointestinal cancers.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Existing image-based biomarker prediction approaches either address different biomarkers (e.g., MSI instead of TMB), are limited to non-gastrointestinal cancers, or fail to rigorously implement multi-instance learning strategies that capture the aggregate label assignment from numerous patch-level predictions. Prior works also neglect stain normalization and systematic evaluation of thresholding for patient-level decisions.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
The prohibitive cost and latency of sequencing-based TMB assessment restrict immunotherapy eligibility for many cancer patients. The ubiquity and low cost of digitized H&E slides, coupled with the recent success of deep learning in computational pathology, motivate the development of non-invasive, accurate TMB prediction directly from histology, potentially democratizing biomarker-driven treatment decisions.

###5. Task Objective:
To develop and evaluate a deep learning and transfer learning–based pipeline that predicts patient-level TMB status (high vs. low) directly from H&E-stained whole slide images of gastrointestinal cancers, achieving clinically relevant accuracy and efficiency without reliance on genomics.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- Kather2019: Predicts microsatellite instability from gastrointestinal histology using end-to-end deep networks, highlighting the feasibility of pathology-based biomarker prediction but not addressing TMB.
- Xu2019: Applies transfer learning on deep CNNs for TMB prediction in bladder cancer by extracting patch-level features; adapts multi-instance learning but not optimized for GI cancer or robust patient-level label fusion.
- ClassicalMIL: Defines bag labels via instance aggregation (max or threshold functions), which is effective for computational tractability but may lose nuanced signal if not all instances contribute equally to the outcome.

##Your Task:

Based on the context above, please generate the following sections for a new research proposal. Be specific, clear, and innovative. Please limit the generated idea to 500 characters.

###1. Idea:
(Based on the above information, please propose an innovative and feasible idea. Include the required professional methods, reasoning, and logical development. Please limit your proposal to 300-500 characters.)

###2. ImplementationSteps:
(Provide a complete and specific list of implementation steps for your idea, and number each step.)

###3. ImplementationOrder:
(Provide a formatted execution route and a checklist of implementation steps. For example, "1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5".)

###4. Dataset:
(Describe the dataset required for the evaluation. If it needs to be created, explain how.)

###5. EvaluationMetrics:
(Define specific, measurable metrics to evaluate the success of the project. Explain why these metrics are relevant.)

###6. ExpectedOutcome:
(Describe the anticipated results and their potential impact on the field. Compare them to the existing solutions.)

First, there is a significant gap in the applicability of existing deep learning models, which predominantly focus on other biomarkers like microsatellite instability (MSI) or are limited to specific cancer types, such as bladder cancer, thus hindering generalization across diverse histological contexts. Second, the implementation of multi-instance learning (MIL) frameworks in histopathology remains suboptimal; classical MIL fails to effectively leverage patch-level contributions, which are critical for accurately representing the complex heterogeneity of tumor microenvironments. This results in a lack of precision in aggregate label assignments necessary for robust biomarker prediction. Additionally, the technical challenge of stain normalization is pivotal, as variations in staining can significantly affect feature extraction and model performance. Lastly, there is insufficient systematic evaluation of thresholding techniques for patient-level decisions, which complicates the translation of model predictions into clinically relevant outcomes. Addressing these challenges is essential for advancing the reliability and applicability of histopathology-based biomarker prediction methodologies in gastrointestinal cancers.
limitation: Existing image-based biomarker prediction approaches either address different biomarkers (e.g., MSI instead of TMB), are limited to non-gastrointestinal cancers, or fail to rigorously implement multi-instance learning strategies that capture the aggregate label assignment from numerous patch-level predictions. Prior works also neglect stain normalization and systematic evaluation of thresholding for patient-level decisions.
motivation: The prohibitive cost and latency of sequencing-based TMB assessment restrict immunotherapy eligibility for many cancer patients. existing_solutions: {'Kather2019': 'Predicts microsatellite instability from gastrointestinal histology using end-to-end deep networks, highlighting the feasibility of pathology-based biomarker prediction but not addressing TMB.', 'Xu2019': 'Applies transfer learning on deep CNNs for TMB prediction in bladder cancer by extracting patch-level features; adapts multi-instance learning but not optimized for GI cancer or robust patient-level label fusion.', 'ClassicalMIL': 'Defines bag labels via instance aggregation (max or threshold functions), which is effective for computational tractability but may lose nuanced signal if not all instances contribute equally to the outcome.'}
core_idea: The authors integrate transfer learning with deep convolutional neural networks to extract discriminative features from color-normalized histology patches, aggregate instance predictions via a threshold-based multi-instance learning scheme for robust patient-level TMB classification, and systematically optimize post-processing thresholds to maximize predictive accuracy on gastric and colon cancer cohorts.
implementation_steps: {'1': 'Acquire and curate TCGA histology images and corresponding somatic mutation data for gastric and colon cancer.', '2': 'Detect tumor regions in WSIs and segment into 224x224 px patches at 0.5 μm/px.', '3': 'Apply Macenko color normalization to all patches.', '4': 'Calculate TMB for each patient from mutation data and assign TMB-high/low labels using the upper tertile rule.', '5': 'Label all patches from each patient with the corresponding TMB label.', '6': 'Split patient data into training and testing cohorts, ensuring no cross-contamination.', '7': 'Preprocess and augment patches (reflection, translation) for model training.', '8': 'Select and fine-tune pre-trained CNN architectures (e.g., GoogLeNet, VGG19) via transfer learning with Adam optimizer and L2 regularization.', '9': 'Predict patch-level TMB probabilities on test set.', '10': 'Aggregate patch-level predictions for each patient using the MIL-based ratio threshold method (default p=0.5).', '11': 'Optimize patient-level decision threshold to maximize accuracy.', '12': 'Evaluate performance using AUC and accuracy on both patch-level and patient-level predictions.'}
data: Two public histopathology datasets from TCGA: (1) TMB-STAD (gastric adenocarcinoma): 280 patients, 217,916 patches; (2) TMB-COAD-DX (colon adenocarcinoma): 265 patients, 154,063 patches. Each sample comprises color-normalized, non-overlapping 224x224 px patches at 0.5 μm/px resolution derived from FFPE H&E-stained slides, with TMB values determined by somatic mutation data and stratified into high/low via upper tertile cutoffs.
evaluation_metrics: {'Area Under the ROC Curve (AUC)': 'Quantifies discriminative ability between TMB-high and TMB-low patients; higher values indicate better classification performance.', 'Accuracy': 'Percentage of correctly classified patients (or patches) at the chosen binarization threshold.', 'Confidence Interval': '95% interval for AUC to assess statistical reliability.', 'Patch-Level vs. Patient-Level Metrics': 'Accuracy computed both at patch granularity and after MIL aggregation at patient level.'}
expected_outcome: The method achieves patient-level AUCs up to 0.75 (TMB-STAD) and 0.82 (TMB-COAD-DX), with optimized classification accuracies of 0.71 and 0.77, respectively. Patch-level accuracies are lower (0.55–0.59). The approach demonstrates the feasibility of predicting TMB directly from H&E slides, offering a cost-effective alternative to sequencing, and reveals potential for stratifying patients for immunotherapy in clinical practice.
related_work_test: {'Using deep learning to predict tumor mutational burden from standard-of-care histology slides across multiple cancer types': 'This study demonstrates that deep learning models can effectively predict TMB from H&E-stained histology slides in several cancer types, offering a non-invasive alternative to genomic sequencing, though model generalizability across datasets remains a challenge.', 'Deep learning approach to predict tumor mutation burden from histopathological images in gastrointestinal cancer': 'This preprint presents a deep learning framework to infer TMB status and its spatial heterogeneity directly from digitized histopathological slides, highlighting the feasibility but noting the need for further validation and robustness improvements.', 'Deep learning-based approach to reveal tumor mutational burden from whole slide images': 'Proposes a weakly-supervised deep learning model using multiple instance learning to estimate TMB from whole slide images, which enables TMB detection without detailed annotation but may be sensitive to variations in staining and scanning protocols.', 'Comparative analysis of machine learning approaches to predict tumor mutational burden in cancer': 'This paper systematically compares various machine learning algorithms for TMB prediction from histology images, finding that deep learning and transfer learning approaches outperform traditional methods, though interpretability remains limited.', 'Deep learning for the detection of microsatellite instability from histology images in colorectal cancer: A systematic literature review': 'Provides a comprehensive review of deep learning applications for detecting microsatellite instability from histopathology, closely related to TMB prediction, and identifies promising results while addressing challenges in clinical translation and reproducibility.'}

question:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- APPS, HumanEval, CodeXGLUE: Benchmarks such as APPS (Hendrycks et al., 2021), HumanEval, and CodeXGLUE assess code generation from natural language, yet focus primarily on isolated code blocks and lack evaluation of end-to-end engineering workflows requiring iterative debugging, navigation of codebases, and resource management.
- SWE-bench: SWE-bench (Jimenez et al., 2024) tasks models with resolving real-world GitHub issues, offering more realistic software engineering evaluation. However, it concentrates on software development and does not capture the practical complexities of ML engineering such as data preparation, model training, and iterative experimentation.
- MLAgentBench: MLAgentBench (Huang et al., 2024b) uses a small set (13) of Kaggle and bespoke ML tasks, evaluating agent improvement over provided baselines. Its limited size and baseline-driven nature make it less representative of diverse, open-ended ML engineering challenges.
- AIDE/Weco AI: AIDE (Schmidt et al., 2024) targets Kaggle competitions, reporting high percentile performance on tabular data tasks. However, its evaluation set is narrower and less diverse than MLE-bench, with less emphasis on high-complexity and non-tabular domains.
- DSBench: DSBench (Jing et al., 2024) benchmarks agents on data science tasks sourced from Kaggle, but filtering criteria restrict tasks to simple formats, excluding many complex, multimodal, or non-standard ML benchmarks present in MLE-bench.

###2. Challenge:
There exists no comprehensive, high-fidelity benchmark to measure the autonomous, end-to-end ML engineering capabilities of AI agents across diverse real-world tasks, including dataset preparation, model development, training, experimentation, and robust evaluation. Existing evaluations focus on narrow code synthesis or simplified ML tasks, lacking breadth, task realism, and direct comparison to human performance at scale.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Previous benchmarks inadequately reflect the open-ended, iterative, and resource-intensive nature of practical ML engineering. They often use artificial baselines, limited task diversity, tabular focus, or neglect crucial aspects such as debugging, data wrangling, modality variety, leaderboard-based human comparison, and agent autonomy in realistic compute environments. Systematic resource, time, and contamination factors are seldom explored.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
The rapid evolution of AI agents capable of complex software and ML workflows necessitates rigorous, human-relevant evaluation of their true engineering abilities. As agentic systems approach human-level automation in ML engineering, understanding their capabilities and limitations becomes critical for responsible deployment, safety preparedness, and monitoring research acceleration risks.

###5. Task Objective:
To design, implement, and open-source a benchmark (MLE-bench) that robustly evaluates AI agents' autonomous performance on real-world, end-to-end ML engineering tasks derived from Kaggle competitions, and to systematically assess their capabilities, limits, and fidelity to human-level achievement.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- APPS, HumanEval, CodeXGLUE: Code generation from description; measures isolated function correctness but omits full experimental pipelines, data engineering, and iterative problem-solving.
- SWE-bench: Real-world software engineering via pull requests; improves realism but targets code fixes rather than ML model development and evaluation complexity.
- MLAgentBench: Few-shot Kaggle/bespoke tasks with agent improvement over baseline; limited scale and no holistic leaderboard-based comparison.
- AIDE/Weco AI: Tree search over tabular Kaggle competitions; optimized for specific task types, lacking broad modality and complexity coverage.
- DSBench: Automated task creation for data science agents; excludes most multimodal, non-tabular, and high-complexity ML tasks due to rigid input requirements.

##Your Task:

Based on the context above, please generate the following sections for a new research proposal. Be specific, clear, and innovative. Please limit the generated idea to 500 characters.

###1. Idea:
(Based on the above information, please propose an innovative and feasible idea. Include the required professional methods, reasoning, and logical development. Please limit your proposal to 300-500 characters.)

###2. ImplementationSteps:
(Provide a complete and specific list of implementation steps for your idea, and number each step.)

###3. ImplementationOrder:
(Provide a formatted execution route and a checklist of implementation steps. For example, "1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5".)

###4. Dataset:
(Describe the dataset required for the evaluation. If it needs to be created, explain how.)

###5. EvaluationMetrics:
(Define specific, measurable metrics to evaluate the success of the project. Explain why these metrics are relevant.)

###6. ExpectedOutcome:
(Describe the anticipated results and their potential impact on the field. Compare them to the existing solutions.)

core_idea: MLE-bench is an open-source benchmark comprising 75 manually curated, diverse Kaggle competitions spanning multiple ML domains and complexities. It evaluates agents on authentic ML engineering workflows—data preprocessing, model training, submission, and iterative improvement—using leaderboard-based human performance as reference, and assesses effects of compute, time, contamination, and resource scaling.
implementation_steps: {'1': 'Curate 75 representative Kaggle competitions, annotate by domain and complexity, and prepare competition assets (description, dataset, grading logic, leaderboard snapshot).', '2': 'Refactor or reconstruct train-test splits where original test sets are unavailable, ensuring distributional comparability to original competitions.', '3': "Implement local grading logic for each competition, mirroring Kaggle's evaluation metrics (e.g., AUROC, MAE, domain-specific losses).", '4': 'Develop the agent execution environment: Docker containers with pre-installed ML libraries, bounded compute (CPU, RAM, GPU), and isolated file systems.', '5': 'Integrate validation servers for submission format checking, without revealing graded scores to agents.', '6': 'Run agents using various scaffolds (AIDE, MLAB, OpenHands) and model backends (GPT-4o, o1-preview, Claude 3.5, Llama 3).', '7': 'Conduct controlled experiments: (a) varying agent scaffolding; (b) varying LLM model; (c) scaling attempts (pass@k); (d) scaling compute (CPU/GPU); (e) scaling allowed runtime.', '8': 'Implement rule-checking and plagiarism detection (Dolos) tools to monitor agent compliance and detect contamination.', '9': 'Aggregate agent outcomes, compare against medal thresholds and private leaderboards, and analyze performance trends.'}
data: MLE-bench consists of 75 diverse Kaggle competitions covering natural language processing, computer vision, tabular, audio, multimodal, and signal processing tasks. Each competition includes: a curated description, a dataset (original or a new, distribution-matched train/test split), local grading code matching the original evaluation metric, and a snapshot of the human leaderboard for contextual comparison. Tasks are annotated by domain and complexity (low/medium/high), with development and test splits.
evaluation_metrics: {'Leaderboard Medal Rate': "Percentage of agent submissions achieving at least bronze medal status based on thresholds mirroring Kaggle's private leaderboard (e.g., top 10%, top 100 teams).", 'Raw Competition Score': "The absolute metric-specific score (e.g., AUROC, MAE) achieved by the agent's submission per task.", 'pass@k': 'Fraction of competitions in which an agent is awarded a medal within k independent attempts, measuring sample efficiency.', 'Submission Validity': 'Percentage of attempts resulting in a valid, properly formatted submission.', 'Resource Usage': 'Compute resource and token consumption (CPU, GPU-hours, input/output tokens) per run.', 'Plagiarism/Contamination Checks': 'Incidence of detected rule violations or code plagiarism via log and code analysis tools.'}
expected_outcome: MLE-bench establishes a rigorous, open benchmark for ML agent engineering, revealing that o1-preview (AIDE) achieves medals in 16.9% of competitions (pass@1), doubling to 34.1% (pass@8). The best agent surpasses median human performance on a significant minority of tasks. Systematic resource scaling and contamination analyses show agent performance is robust to hardware and instruction obfuscation, and not systematically inflated by memorization. The benchmark enables reproducible, human-relevant assessment and is expected to drive advances in autonomous ML agent research.

question:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- AutoGluonTabular [24]: Ensembles multiple models and stacking strategies to automate tabular ML pipelines. Achieves strong performance with minimal user intervention, but is restricted to static search spaces and lacks adaptability to new tasks and feedback.
- AutoML-GPT [26]: Prompts LLMs to automate the ML pipeline, reducing manual efforts. However, relies on static prompt engineering, resulting in limited adaptability and does not improve through experience.
- AIDE [16]: Reframes ML engineering as code optimization using trial-and-error strategies and tree search for solution refinement. Provides iterative improvements, but lacks an explicit learning mechanism from previous experiences, making strategies static and prompt-dependent.
- MLCopilot [27]: Utilizes LLMs with past experience retrieval to automate ML, but does not train agents on those experiences, leaving strategies unoptimized across tasks.
- DeepSeek-R1 [39]: A massive 671B parameter LLM agent trained with RL to incentivize reasoning. Excels in complex reasoning but incurs high computational cost and is not explicitly designed for agentic ML engineering.

###2. Challenge:
The core challenges in the research area of autonomous machine learning using large language models (LLMs) stem from several technical limitations inherent in current methodologies. First, the reliance on static prompt engineering constrains adaptability and fails to leverage the dynamic nature of machine learning tasks, resulting in suboptimal performance when faced with evolving datasets or requirements. Second, the lack of mechanisms for experiential learning and iterative self-improvement means that existing systems do not refine their strategies based on past performances, leading to stagnation and ineffective exploration of potential solutions. This is compounded by the absence of a cohesive reward structure which could guide the optimization of agents across diverse tasks. Furthermore, the high computational costs associated with large models, such as DeepSeek-R1, pose significant barriers to scalability and practical deployment, particularly in resource-limited environments. Consequently, these challenges hinder the development of robust, flexible, and efficient autonomous ML systems capable of tackling novel and complex problems.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Prior approaches to autonomous ML with LLM agents heavily depend on static prompt engineering and do not optimize agents via learning from experimentation. They fail to capture the generalization potential and iterative self-improvement of agents, suffer from poor exploration, slow feedback loops, and lack a unified reward structure, resulting in limited scalability and adaptability to novel or complex ML tasks.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
The vision of fully autonomous ML engineering demands agents capable of iteratively learning from diverse task-solving experiences, efficiently exploring solution spaces, and generalizing across tasks and domains. Current prompt-based LLM agents are inherently static, unable to harness their interaction history for policy improvement. Addressing this bottleneck is crucial for scalable, robust, and adaptive AI-driven ML engineering.

###5. Task Objective:
To develop and train a large language model (LLM) agent capable of autonomous machine learning engineering, wherein the agent iteratively interacts with ML environments, adapts its policy based on feedback through online reinforcement learning, and generalizes to new ML tasks with minimal human intervention.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- Prompt-based LLM Agents (e.g., AIDE, MLCopilot): Rely on manually crafted prompts to guide action selection in ML environments. While able to automate some ML tasks and leverage LLM coding abilities, strategies remain static, are brittle to environment changes, and require significant human oversight and iterative prompt tuning.
- Tree-search/Expert-based Agents (e.g., AIDE): Use search or expert trajectories to iteratively refine code and actions, achieving some degree of iterative improvement. However, they do not incorporate learning from prior experience into the agent's policy, resulting in limited adaptability and static behavior.
- RL-Enhanced LLMs (e.g., DeepSeek-R1, AgentQ): Train LLMs using RL to improve alignment and reasoning. These methods target general language or reasoning tasks and do not address the specific challenges of agentic ML environments, such as action diversity, slow feedback, and reward signal heterogeneity.

##Your Task:

Based on the context above, please generate the following sections for a new research proposal. Be specific, clear, and innovative. Please limit the generated idea to 500 characters.

###1. Idea:
(Based on the above information, please propose an innovative and feasible idea. Include the required professional methods, reasoning, and logical development. Please limit your proposal to 300-500 characters.)

###2. ImplementationSteps:
(Provide a complete and specific list of implementation steps for your idea, and number each step.)

###3. ImplementationOrder:
(Provide a formatted execution route and a checklist of implementation steps. For example, "1-2", "2-3", "3-4", "4-5".)

###4. Dataset:
(Describe the dataset required for the evaluation. If it needs to be created, explain how.)

###5. EvaluationMetrics:
(Define specific, measurable metrics to evaluate the success of the project. Explain why these metrics are relevant.)

###6. ExpectedOutcome:
(Describe the anticipated results and their potential impact on the field. Compare them to the existing solutions.)

expected_outcome: ML-Agent, a 7B-parameter LLM agent, achieves state-of-the-art or superior performance on 13 ML tasks—including 10 unseen held-out tasks—outperforming the 671B DeepSeek-R1 and other baseline agents. On held-in tasks, ML-Agent achieves up to 18% relative improvement; on held-out tasks, up to 16% improvement. Step-wise RL reduces RL training time while improving generalization and learning efficiency. The framework enables continuous self-improvement, robust cross-task generalization, and high action diversity, setting a new standard for autonomous ML engineering.

question:
You are a top-tier researcher in your field. Based on the following context, please generate a novel and detailed research proposal.

##Context:

###1. Related Work:
- ReAct (Yao et al., 2023): A general-purpose LLM agent framework that interleaves natural language reasoning with action selection, storing the entire interaction history in the prompt. This enables flexible task execution but suffers from prompt length limitations and lacks principled decomposition of structured tasks.
- AutoML (Feurer et al., 2015, 2020; LeDell and Poirier, 2020a; Olson and Moore, 2016): Automated frameworks for model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and pipeline configuration using Bayesian optimization, meta-learning, and ensemble methods. These approaches rely on static, predefined search spaces and lack dynamic adaptability for code-level innovation.
- Neural Architecture Search (Zoph and Le, 2017; Liu et al., 2019; Real et al., 2019): Automates the search for neural network topologies via reinforcement learning or differentiable optimization. While effective for architecture design, these methods are computationally expensive and also require manually defined search spaces.
- Voyager (Wang et al., 2023) and AlphaCode (Li et al., 2022): Domain-specialized agents for code generation and embodied reasoning, integrating execution feedback for iterative refinement. They excel in narrow domains but do not generalize to broad ML engineering tasks or support dynamic code-based exploration.

###2. Challenge:
Automating the iterative, trial-and-error process of machine learning engineering, which is typically laborious, compute-intensive, and reliant on human expertise for code refinement and optimization. Existing automated ML and LLM-based agents are constrained by static search spaces or monolithic optimization approaches, leading to suboptimal exploration efficiency, limited scalability, and prompt context limitations. Balancing the efficient reuse and refinement of promising code solutions in a manner that enables stateful, incremental improvement without overwhelming LLM context windows.

###3. Limitations of Existing Approaches:
Prior approaches to AutoML and LLM-driven agents depend on static configuration spaces and prompt-based context accumulation, resulting in inefficiency, a lack of scalable iterative refinement, limited adaptability to code-level modifications, and frequent failure to outperform human experts in complex ML engineering tasks.

###4. Motivation for New Research:
With ML engineering workflows increasingly bottlenecked by iterative code optimization and trial-and-error, there is a pressing need for automated systems that can efficiently explore and refine code solutions, leveraging LLMs' reasoning and coding capabilities, while maintaining sample efficiency and scalability across diverse, real-world benchmarks.

###5. Task Objective:
To develop an LLM-powered agent that frames machine learning engineering as an optimization problem in code space, and systematically explores, drafts, debugs, and refines code solutions via a structured tree search—achieving human-competitive or superior performance on ML engineering benchmarks including Kaggle, MLE-Bench, and RE-Bench.

###6. Existing Solutions:
- AutoML systems: Rely on predefined model and hyperparameter spaces with Bayesian or evolutionary search, offering robust baseline automation but limited flexibility for code-level innovation and inefficient in adapting to new problem structures.
- ReAct-style LLM agents: Accumulate all interaction history in prompt context; provide general reasoning and action abilities but become intractable for long tasks and are not tailored for incremental, modular code improvement.
- Neural Architecture Search: Employs RL or differentiable search for network topologies, focused on architecture rather than end-to-end ML pipeline code, and incurs high computational costs with limited interpretability and extensibility.
- Human-assisted LLM workflows: Enhance productivity but still depend heavily on human orchestration for iterative improvement and debugging, limiting automation and scalability.

related_work: {'ReAct (Yao et al., 2023)': 'A general-purpose LLM agent framework that interleaves natural language reasoning with action selection, storing the entire interaction history in the prompt. This enables flexible task execution but suffers from prompt length limitations and lacks principled decomposition of structured tasks.', 'AutoML (Feurer et al., 2015, 2020; LeDell and Poirier, 2020a; Olson and Moore, 2016)': 'Automated frameworks for model selection, hyperparameter tuning, and pipeline configuration using Bayesian optimization, meta-learning, and ensemble methods. These approaches rely on static, predefined search spaces and lack dynamic adaptability for code-level innovation.', 'Neural Architecture Search (Zoph and Le, 2017; Liu et al., 2019; Real et al., 2019)': 'Automates the search for neural network topologies via reinforcement learning or differentiable optimization. While effective for architecture design, these methods are computationally expensive and also require manually defined search spaces.', 'Voyager (Wang et al., 2023) and AlphaCode (Li et al., 2022)': 'Domain-specialized agents for code generation and embodied reasoning, integrating execution feedback for iterative refinement. They excel in narrow domains but do not generalize to broad ML engineering tasks or support dynamic code-based exploration.'}
challenge: Automating the iterative, trial-and-error process of machine learning engineering, which is typically laborious, compute-intensive, and reliant on human expertise for code refinement and optimization. Existing automated ML and LLM-based agents are constrained by static search spaces or monolithic optimization approaches, leading to suboptimal exploration efficiency, limited scalability, and prompt context limitations. Balancing the efficient reuse and refinement of promising code solutions in a manner that enables stateful, incremental improvement without overwhelming LLM context windows.
limitation: Prior approaches to AutoML and LLM-driven agents depend on static configuration spaces and prompt-based context accumulation, resulting in inefficiency, a lack of scalable iterative refinement, limited adaptability to code-level modifications, and frequent failure to outperform human experts in complex ML engineering tasks.
motivation: With ML engineering workflows increasingly bottlenecked by iterative code optimization and trial-and-error, there is a pressing need for automated systems that can efficiently explore and refine code solutions, leveraging LLMs' reasoning and coding capabilities, while maintaining sample efficiency and scalability across diverse, real-world benchmarks.
task_objective: To develop an LLM-powered agent that frames machine learning engineering as an optimization problem in code space, and systematically explores, drafts, debugs, and refines code solutions via a structured tree search—achieving human-competitive or superior performance on ML engineering benchmarks including Kaggle, MLE-Bench, and RE-Bench.
existing_solutions: {'AutoML systems': 'Rely on predefined model and hyperparameter spaces with Bayesian or evolutionary search, offering robust baseline automation but limited flexibility for code-level innovation and inefficient in adapting to new problem structures.', 'ReAct-style LLM agents': 'Accumulate all interaction history in prompt context; provide general reasoning and action abilities but become intractable for long tasks and are not tailored for incremental, modular code improvement.', 'Neural Architecture Search': 'Employs RL or differentiable search for network topologies, focused on architecture rather than end-to-end ML pipeline code, and incurs high computational costs with limited interpretability and extensibility.', 'Human-assisted LLM workflows': 'Enhance productivity but still depend heavily on human orchestration for iterative improvement and debugging, limiting automation and scalability.'}
core_idea: AIDE introduces a tree search–based LLM agent that frames ML engineering as code optimization. It decomposes trial-and-error into atomic code revisions—drafting, debugging, and improving—while recording all solutions in a solution tree. Specialized prompts and concise summarization enable scalable, stateless exploration, leading to robust, competitive results across ML tasks.
implementation_steps: {'1': 'Initialize the solution tree with a base (empty or default) code solution.', '2': 'Select a node for refinement based on search policy: draft new, debug existing, or improve upon the best.', '3': 'Generate a new solution using the coding operator with a tailored LLM prompt (plan, implement, or atomically revise code).', '4': 'Evaluate the candidate solution using an objective function (e.g., local metric, leaderboard score) to obtain scalar performance.', '5': 'Update the solution tree by adding the new node, recording code, score, and modification provenance.', '6': 'Apply the summarization operator to extract key metrics and debugging info, ensuring prompt conciseness.', '7': 'Iterate: Use updated solution tree and summarization to drive the next round of selection and generation.', '8': 'At termination, return the solution with the highest evaluation score.'}
data: AIDE is evaluated on (i) Weco-Kaggle: 63 Kaggle competitions of varied complexity and data size (including tabular ML, image, and time-series), with a focused subset of 16 tabular tasks ('Weco-Kaggle Lite'); (ii) MLE-Bench: 75 real Kaggle competitions (diverse ML domains) for offline agent evaluation; (iii) RE-Bench: 7 complex AI R&D tasks (e.g., Triton kernel optimization, GPT-2 finetuning), benchmarking against human experts. Data structure comprises standard train/test splits, leaderboard scores, and metadata for coding prompts.
evaluation_metrics: {'Exceeds % of Humans': 'The mean percentage of human competition participants outperformed by the agent, estimated from leaderboard quantiles.', 'Above Median': "Fraction of competitions where the agent's solution outperforms the human median (leaderboard median).", 'Valid Submission Rate': 'Proportion of agent submissions passing all validity checks on the target competition platform.', 'Any Medal Rate': 'Fraction of tasks where the agent achieves a bronze, silver, or gold medal (as per competition thresholds); primary success criterion in MLE-Bench.', 'Gold Rate': 'Fraction of tasks where the solution attains the highest medal tier.', 'Pass@k': 'Probability of producing at least one valid or medal-winning solution within k independent attempts.', 'Task-specific metrics': 'Competition-defined metrics (e.g., RMSE, AUC, accuracy) used for local evaluation and leaderboard rank.'}
expected_outcome: AIDE achieves an average Exceeds % of Humans score of 51.38% and Above Median of 50% on Weco-Kaggle Lite, outperforming major AutoML and LLM agent baselines. On MLE-Bench, AIDE+o1-preview attains 16.9% Any Medal (vs. 4.4% for OpenHands) and 29.4% Above Median. On RE-Bench, AIDE surpasses human expert averages within six hours on complex AI R&D tasks. Performance improvements include a fivefold increase in medal rates versus prior agents, robust scalability, and reduced manual intervention or cost per task.
